notes.note: Gaius Trebatius Testa, the jurist whom Cicero had commended to Caesar in Gaul and the recipient of the affectionate Ad Familiares book 7; by now a close friend in Cicero's circle. Later one of the great jurisconsults of the age.
A visitor named Curtius who called to pay his respects. He cannot be securely identified with any of the several Curtii already in the registry.
Marcus Tullius Tiro, Cicero's freedman and confidential secretary, who took dictation and ran his literary affairs. The point is that even his most trusted amanuensis must not hear the matter.

notes.anchor: It came round, however, to Quintus
notes.note: Quintus Cicero, the orator's younger brother; the talk turned to the bitter family rift of these years between the brothers and the younger Quintus. The "unspeakable" matter is the substance of that quarrel.

Lucius Manlius Torquatus, a friend of Cicero whose legal or personal business Cicero had taken up; he is the Epicurean spokesman of Cicero's De Finibus book 1. Dolabella reports to him the care Cicero gave his case.

notes.anchor: Nicias was thinking the thing settled
notes.note: Curtius Nicias of Cos, a learned grammarian and friend in the circle of Cicero, Atticus, and Dolabella. Here he is a source of news about Brutus's domestic affairs.
Arpinum, the hill town of the Volsci in southern Latium that was Cicero's birthplace and where he held family estates. He needs to settle the rents on those small properties.
A letter of one of the Valerii Messallae from which Atticus had inferred the timing of Caesar's return to Italy. Which Messalla is meant cannot be determined with certainty.
